evals.name: rust-express-with-capstone
evals.prompt: teach me Rust. I'm a backend dev with 5 years of Go, comfortable with terminals. Just use your standard format, I trust your defaults. One thing though: I want a real capstone project at the end, something I could actually put on GitHub.
evals.expected_output: Express path: cost estimate first, brief Phase 0 + Phase 1 confirmation, single-paragraph format confirmation, then a mockup/ directory with three working files (dashboard, sample-lesson, sample-tool), pausing for review before full build. Final curriculum (if built) should include an opt-in capstone.
expectations: Cost estimate appears as the very first output, before any planning
Express path is taken — no extensive collaborative format brainstorming
A mockup/ directory exists with dashboard.html, sample-lesson.html, and sample-tool.html
All three mockup files are fully styled (real CSS, not placeholder)
sample-tool.html contains a working interactive code editor (CodeMirror or styled textarea) for Rust
The agent pauses for mockup feedback before generating the full module repo
Curriculum plan or repo includes a capstone project (since the user asked for one)
Lightweight check-ins (quizzes / mini-challenges / reflection) appear in the plan, not just modules + capstone

evals.name: watercolor-tailored-no-capstone
evals.prompt: teach me watercolor. I want to co-design this with you — I have opinions. No streaks, no gamification, no big final project (I get demotivated by looming things). I want long-form lesson pages, not paginated. Visual style should feel like a quiet sketchbook, not a video game.
evals.expected_output: Tailored path: cost estimate first, brief Phase 0/1, then collaborative brainstorming on a few format axes (lesson layout, tool, tone, visual identity) with concrete proposals — not vague questions. Mockup reflects the no-gamification, long-form, sketchbook-aesthetic preferences. No capstone. Subtle check-ins (reflection prompts, not quizzes with scores).
expectations: Cost estimate appears as the very first output
Tailored path is taken — agent opens collaborative brainstorming, not a one-paragraph confirmation
Brainstorming offers concrete proposals with tradeoffs (e.g., 'I'd lean toward X because Y, alternatives are Z'), not open-ended 'how would you like it?'
A mockup/ directory exists with dashboard.html, sample-lesson.html, and sample-tool.html
Lessons in the mockup are long-form / single-scroll, not paginated
Visual identity is muted/sketchbook (warm neutrals, paper-like backgrounds, hand-drawn feel) — not vibrant or game-like
No streaks, scores, badges, or progress points anywhere in the mockup or curriculum
No capstone project in the curriculum plan
Check-ins are subtle — reflection prompts or self-assessments, not graded quizzes

evals.name: photography-vague-needs-probing
evals.prompt: teach me photography. I'm a complete beginner.
evals.expected_output: Cost estimate first, then Phase 0 questions woven in, then Phase 1 interview that explicitly surfaces the format-depth branching question (express vs. tailored). Should not silently pick a path.
expectations: Cost estimate appears as the very first output
Phase 0 questions (scope, budget, seriousness, platform) are surfaced
Phase 1 interview asks about how the learner likes to learn (style, tone, motivation)
The format-depth question is explicitly asked — express vs. tailored — not silently assumed
The assessment-rhythm question is asked — capstone vs. steady check-ins
Agent does not build any files before getting answers to Phase 0 / Phase 1

evals.name: mockup-iteration-feedback
evals.prompt:
[Mid-flow context: We're already past Phase 0, 1, and 2. The user chose the tailored path for learning embedded systems / Arduino. You already built the following mockup files in mockup/:
- mockup/dashboard.html — a dashboard with module list and progress bars
- mockup/sample-lesson.html — a long lesson page with dense paragraphs of theory
- mockup/sample-tool.html — a circuit simulator widget

The user has now opened the mockup and replies:]

Love the dashboard, but the lesson page feels way too dense — can you make it more breathable and add a recurring narrator character? Maybe like a friendly engineer who pops in with side comments.
evals.expected_output: The agent should iterate on the mockup files specifically — editing mockup/sample-lesson.html (and possibly the others for consistency) to add a recurring narrator and reduce density. It should NOT begin building the full module repo. After editing, it should re-prompt for feedback or confirmation.
expectations: Agent edits files inside mockup/ rather than creating new module-* directories
mockup/sample-lesson.html is modified — density reduced (more whitespace, shorter paragraphs, visual breathing room)
A recurring narrator character is added — appears at least twice in the lesson with side comments / asides
The narrator has a consistent visual treatment (an avatar, a callout box, a distinct style) — not just italics
Agent does NOT build the full repo (no module-01, module-02 directories with real lessons)
Agent asks for further feedback or confirms the iteration is good before moving on
